coredata.dc:title: Visible light active N, S-Carbon quantum dots@FeVO4 nanocomposite as a photodegradation mechanism for wastewater treatment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yousaf, Muhammad Imran
Mansha, Hafsa
Habiba, Umme
Kiran, Misbah
Javid, Amjed
coredata.dc:description: Environmental remediation involves various strategies, such as degradation and related techniques to reduce the risks posed by chemical and radiological contaminants to human health and ecosystems. The use of nanomaterials in remediation provides advantages including enhanced efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and rapid pollutant breakdown. In this study, an innovative photocatalyst based on Iron Vanadate and Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (FeVO4/N, S-CQDs) was synthesized via a hydrothermal route using varying concentrations of N, S-CQDs. Structural characterization using X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirmed the formation of a pure anorthic phase of FeVO4 after calcination at 300 °C for 180 min, with no impurity peaks and a crystallite size of 29 nm. Photocatalytic activity was evaluated using Methylene Orange (MO) as a model dye under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm). The sample with 0.05 wt% N, S-CQDs showed the highest degradation efficiency, achieving up to 99 % dye removal. UV–Vis spectroscopy revealed over 80 % MO degradation using a small amount of catalyst, with kinetic analysis indicating a pseudo-first-order reaction. SEM analysis demonstrated a reduction in particle size from 4 µm to 200 nm with N, S-CQD incorporation, while EDX confirmed elemental composition. The nanocomposite also showed high reusability with negligible loss in photocatalytic activity. These findings indicate that FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposites are effective, stable, and promising materials for the treatment of organic contaminants in industrial wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FeVO4
N, S-CQDs
Photocatalytic activity
Nanocomposite
Wastewater degradation
Azo dyes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FeVO4, N, S-CQDs and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs synthesis.
(a) XRD patterns of FeVO4, FeVO4/CQDs with different concentrations (b) FTIR patterns of CQDs, FeVO4, and FeVO4/CQDs with different concentrations.
SEM images of (a) FeVO4 (b) FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.10 wt%) (c) FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.05 wt%) (d) EDS of FeVO4/N,S-CQDs (0.05 wt%).
(a) Degradation concentration, (b) Degradation of FeVO4, (c) Degradation of FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.05 wt%), (d) Degradation of FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.10 wt%), (e) Degradation of FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.15 wt%).
UV absorption spectra of (a) pure FeVO4 (b) FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.05 wt%). (c) FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.10 wt%) (d) FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.15 wt%)
Degradation of MO dye with (a) FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs, (b) degradation on different pH.
(a) Effect of FeVO4/N, S-CQDs concentration on the degradation of MO, (b) pH effect on the photodegradation of MO dyes.
Photocatalytic decoloration of methyl orange with different dye concentrations.
Determination of the degradation% %, rate constant (k), half-life time (t1/2), as well as determination coefficient (R2).

head.ce:title: Visible light active N, S-Carbon quantum dots@FeVO4 nanocomposite as a photodegradation mechanism for wastewater treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Sciences, National Textile University, Faisalabad, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Sciences, National Textile University, Faisalabad, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Textile Engineering, National Textile University, Faisalabad, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Textile Engineering, National Textile University, Faisalabad, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmental remediation involves various strategies, such as degradation and related techniques to reduce the risks posed by chemical and radiological contaminants to human health and ecosystems. The use of nanomaterials in remediation provides advantages including enhanced efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and rapid pollutant breakdown. In this study, an innovative photocatalyst based on Iron Vanadate and Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (FeVO4/N, S-CQDs) was synthesized via a hydrothermal route using varying concentrations of N, S-CQDs. Structural characterization using X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirmed the formation of a pure anorthic phase of FeVO4 after calcination at 300 °C for 180 min, with no impurity peaks and a crystallite size of 29 nm. Photocatalytic activity was evaluated using Methylene Orange (MO) as a model dye under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm). The sample with 0.05 wt% N, S-CQDs showed the highest degradation efficiency, achieving up to 99 % dye removal. UV–Vis spectroscopy revealed over 80 % MO degradation using a small amount of catalyst, with kinetic analysis indicating a pseudo-first-order reaction. SEM analysis demonstrated a reduction in particle size from 4 µm to 200 nm with N, S-CQD incorporation, while EDX confirmed elemental composition. The nanocomposite also showed high reusability with negligible loss in photocatalytic activity. These findings indicate that FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposites are effective, stable, and promising materials for the treatment of organic contaminants in industrial wastewater.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and Sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Azo dyes are the dominant category of synthetic organic dyes, with over 50 % of all widely used dyes being azo dyes [1]. Therefore, removing them from water and wastewater is crucial before discharging them into natural water resources. In our worldwide marine environment, one of the major problems is organic pollution of water bodies [2,3]. Transition metal-based orthovanadates (AVO4) are a cohesive class of materials that have potential applications in various fields and can be used as catalysts, cathode materials in lithium-ion batteries, gas sensors, solar cells, photoluminescence, and optical fibers. FeVO4 is a highly stable and highly selective catalyst that can be used in various applications such as photo catalytic degradation of the organic pollutants, gas sensor materials in detecting H2S trace in the air environment, and electrochromic electrodes [4]. Carbon Quantum Dotshave remarkable fluorescence capabilities and consist of ultra-fine, distributed, quasi-spherical carbonnanoparticles. It not only overcomes some deficiencies of traditional organic dyes and nanomaterials, but also has superior performance than traditional semiconductorquantum dots, including small molecular weight, low toxicity, simplesurface functionalization, diverse sources of raw materials, low cost, tiny particle size, excellent fluorescence stability, adjustable emission wavelength, and strong biocompatibility [5].

FeVO4 exists in four polymorphs typically generated at elevated temperatures and pressures. Among these, FeVO4-I is stable at room temperature, while FeVO4-II, III, and IV are metastable. The crystal structure of FeVO4-I is triclinic, while FeVO4-II, III, and IV have orthorhombic, orthorhombic a-PbO2, and monoclinic wolframite NiWO4 structures, respectively [6]. FeVO4 is a vital component of ash and has interesting properties and uses, including in lithium-ion battery catalysts, cathode materials, gas sensors, solar cells, photoluminescence, and microwave applications [7].

Quantum graphene dots (QGDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel forms of carbon nanomaterials smaller than 10 nm and have fascinating properties. Doping FeVO4 with N, S-CQDs can enhance the photocatalytic activity of FeVO4 and make it more reactive. Carbon-based quantum dots have tremendous uses in bioimaging, biosensors, biomolecule/drug delivery, and other fields due to their exceptional biological features, such as less toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [8]. Photoluminescent carbon-based quantum dots have higher levels than typical semiconductor quantum dots or organic dyes regarding good aqueous solubility, chemical inertness, comfort modification, as well as photobleaching resistance. N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanocomposite shows the best photocatalytic results were obtained with 0.05 g of the photocatalyst per 50 ml of a 50 ppm MB dye solution, and the prepared N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanocomposite showed excellent stability after four reaction cycles [9]. The synthesized S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite possessed great stability with only 23 % loss of activity after 6 reaction cycles, and it gives a promising alternative for organic pollutant degradation applications [10]. Thephotoluminescence spectrumof FeVO4-NPs showed the potential in the red region ofvisible light, and it effectively catalyzes the reaction initiator (H2O2 ∼ 0.05 ml dose) to produce strong OH• radicals with 98.74 %photocatalyticdegradation performance. The photo-induced degradation activity was efficiently improved for low initiator concentration, and FeVO4-NPs are helpful for Fenton-like photocatalytic degradation of thewater pollutants [11]. N-GQD/ZnO composite showed higher elimination performance, by which the MB dye could be removed completely. The best nanophotocatalyst demonstrated maximum dye removal efficiency (70 % in about 30 min, 100 % < 60 min). Thus, nano photocatalysts of similar characteristics are suggested for industrial effluent purification under visible light [12].

Carbon-based quantum dots show excellent electronic properties as electron donors and receivers, leading to vast possibilities in the field of photoelectric, catalysis, and sensors [13]. This study aims to synthesize FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposites for the photocatalytic decomposition of azo dyes. Since pure semiconductors are not efficient photocatalysts, N, S-CQDs doping can enhance the photocatalytic activity of FeVO4 or make it more reactive. Due to the low adsorption efficiency, the photocatalytic activity of pure iron vanadate is low; therefore, N, S-CQDs are added to solve this problem. This not only improves photocatalytic activity but also improves structural stability and prevents photo corrosion and dissolution in water. Due to their intriguing photophysical properties, small size, biocompatibility, and chemical inertness, carbon dots (CDs) have been employed in different applications such as bio-imaging, optical nano sensing, electrochemical analysis, drug delivery, pH meter, and as an antibacterial agent [14,15]. The exceptional electronic properties of these quantum dots enable chemiluminescence and electrochemiluminescence and allow them to function as both electron donors and receivers, creating exciting possibilities in the fields of photo-electronics, catalysis, and sensing. Recent studies have focused on co-precipitation processes to synthesize carbonaceous materials with non-metallic elements. We are now interested in learning more about the properties of co-doped carbon dots with metallic elements. Because of this, it is anticipated that adding chemically bound metallic elements will change the electronic structure of carbon nanomaterials, resulting in more desirable photophysical and surprising analytical properties.

The used chemicals are Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH) having CAS number of 1310–73-2, Ascorbic acid (Ethanol) having CAS number of 50–81-7, Iron Nitrate Nona Hydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) having CAS number of 7782–61-8, Ammonium Meta Vanadate (NH4VO3) having CAS number of 7803–55-6, L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S) having CAS number of 52–90-4, citric acid (C6H8O7) having CAS number of 77–92-9, and distilled water having CAS number of 7732–18-5. All these chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.

First, 7.096 g of Fe (NO3)3·9H2O were mixed in 50 mL distilled water, to get a homogenous mixture, and stirred on a magnetic stirrer for about 30 min. The second solution, which contains 2.052 g NH4VO3 and 50 mL of distilled water, is stirred for 30 min. After that, using a pipette, drops of the NH4VO3 solution were added to the Fe (NO3)3·9H2O second solution. Then the base solution NaOH was combined to get the pH value between 8 and 9, indicating completion of the reaction. After that further solution was stirred for one hour and transferred to the autoclave for 12 h at 180 °C. Then the final product from the autoclave kept settling down the particles.

The NS-CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. 2 g of CA and 1 g of TSC were combined with 150 mL of ultrapure water. Then the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (250 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After that, the autoclave was cooled at room temperature (RT), and the obtained solution was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was removed and purified through a 0.22-μm syringe filter. Then the NS-CQDs solution was obtained.

Firstly, the synthesized N, S-CQDs were mixed with 30 mL of distilled water and stirred for 30 min. Under continuous stirring for 30 min, this homogenous mixture was added drop by drop to the previous solution of FeVO4 with different concentrations (0.05, 0.10, 0.15 wt%). The resulting combination was placed in a stainless-steel autoclave lined with Teflon, where it was kept at 140 °C for 12 h. After the reaction finished, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally. The synthesized yellowish suspension was cleaned with ethanol, and distilled water was dried at 60 °C for 8 h. A complete schematic synthesis diagram is shown in Fig. 1 .

A UV–Visible absorbance spectrometer from Shimadzu Japan was used to analyze the absorbance spectra of the N, S-CQDs@FeVO4 to investigate the optical properties of the composite. The UV–Visible (UV–vis, UV-3600) absorbance spectrometer confirms that the N, S-CQDs@FeVO4 nanocomposite is photo-catalytically efficient. A diffractometer with an X-ray spectrum (Bruker, D8 ADVANCE) was used to investigate the material’s crystalline structures in the 2θ range of 20◦–80◦. Moreover, the functional groups of N, S-CQDs, FeVO4, and N, S-CQDs@FeVO4 were determined by using a Shimadzu Varian 4300 spectrophotometer, which operates within the frequency range of 600–4000 cm−1. Additionally, the surface morphology and phase composition of FeVO4 and N, S-CQDs@FeVO4 were determined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI SU8220), and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis on a Hitachi SU8220.

To analyze the crystalline structure of FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposites using XRD at an angle 2θ ranging from 20°-80°. Using Cu-K radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) and a high-resolution X-ray diffractometer, the XRD results of FeVO4 nanoparticles were examined. The obtained anorthic phase of FeVO4 nanoparticles displayed broad reflection peaks, indicating a fine particle nature (Fig. 2 a). The FeVO4 nanoparticles' predicted cell parameters were a = 8.4210 Å, b = 8.0600 Å, and c = 9.2540 Å. The phase purity and crystallinity of the synthesized FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanoparticles were investigated using powder XRD measurements. Indicating phase purity, the undoped FeVO4 nanoparticles displayed peaks that matched those seen for triclinic FeVO4 (JCPDS#01–075-1519) (Fig. 2a). Their powder XRD patterns further demonstrated their purity by showing no impurity peaks. The Scherer equation determined that the average crystalline size of FeVO4 nanoparticles was 29 nm. Except for a slight variation in peak intensity to lower angle values, the XRD patterns of the NSCQD-doped nanocomposites were like those of the parent host chemical. The XRD analysis revealed a diffraction peak of FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.05 wt%) composite patterns with lower FeVO4 content. This may be due to a decrease in the diffraction peak intensity of pure FeVO4. Moreover, the EDX examination showed that these samples contained FeVO4 [9,15].

Fig. 2b represents the IR spectrum of N, S-CQDs, where the peaks at 1643 cm−1, 1032 cm−1, as well as 873 cm−1, correspond to stretching vibrations of C–C, C–H, as well as C-O/C-O-C groups, respectively. The broad band at approximately 970 cm−1 is attributed to the terminal V-O stretching at the end of the tetrahedral position. The bands at 881 cm−1, as well as 801 cm−1, are attributed to pass-over V-O-Fe stretching. Furthermore, the strong bands at 2942 cm−1 and 1727 cm−1 correspond to the vibrational modes of the water molecules absorbed on the nanoparticle's surface. Notably, no peak near 3424 cm−1, attributed to the OH group in the FT-IR spectrum, was observed in the FeVO4 sample. Fig. 2b presents the FT-IR of the major IR peaks in the FeVO4 and N, S-CQDs spectra, which were also present in the FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposite spectra. Additionally, the characteristic peak at 1437 cm−1 in the infrared spectrum of the nanocomposite FeVO4/N, S-CQDs moved to 1495 cm−1, indicating an interaction between N, S-CQDs and FeVO4 in the nanocomposite [11,12].

The surface morphology and roughness of a high-density sample, which is like spherical particles measuring 45 nm in diameter, are visible in the SEM images. Fig. 3 a micrographs show that the prepared FeVO4 is made up of nanoplates of polyhedrons. Fig. 3b depicts the SEM image of the freshly made FeVO4/N, S-CQDs(0.10 wt%) photocatalyst, demonstrating the presence of numerous FeVO4 nanoplates and crystals as well as the successful preparation of the FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposite photocatalyst. The experimental conditions, the reaction mechanism, and the material composition all have a role in how the polyhedron plate forms and changes. Fig. 3c shows the SEM image of optimized composite FeVO4/N, S-CQDs(0.05 wt%) with particle morphology, indicating the presence of agglomerations of particles [16,17]. SEM observation indicates that the attachment of quantum dots reduces the size of the particles from 4 µm to 1 µm, and further addition of 0.05 wt% N, S-CQDs reduce the size to 200 nm. FeVO4 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots are adjacent to each other, aiding in the transport of photo-generated electrons in FeVO4.

To evaluate the final products' chemical composition and purity, an EDS analysis was conducted as shown in Fig. 3d. Fe, O, and V elements can all be found in FeVO4 nanoparticles based on their EDS spectra. The lack of N emissions in the EDS spectrum further proves the product's purity and absence of contaminants. Table 1 includes a list of the compounds used to make composites. All elemental compositions are according to the design of the experiment [18].

The photodegradation of organic dyes such as methylene orange (MO) according to ultraviolet (UV) as well as visible light irradiation has been assessed for the photocatalytic capabilities of FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs at various concentrations. The chemical structure of MO used as a model dye is shown in Fig. 4 . Although MO has many industrial, biological, and medical uses, its open emissions into the water from industry pose serious harm to the environment. As the irradiation time interval is increased, a gradual red shift in wavelength is noted at the absorption peak at 460 nm. This implies that the ethyl groups in MO dye are gradually removed over time as it becomes less ethylated [19]. To verify dye adsorption, samples were tested at 0 min and left soaked in darkness, then exposed to a light source (430 nm visible). Within twenty minutes, the solution samples were examined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to gauge the degree of MO deterioration. This procedure was carried out for 120 min again [20]. The photocatalytic degradation percentage was calculated by the following equation:
Degradation Efficiency (%) = Co-C/Co × 100 %.

The photocatalytic performance of FeVO4, FeVO4/N, S-CQDs composite at 0.05 wt% concentration, FeVO4/N, S-CQDs composite at 0.10 wt% concentration, and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs composite at 0.15 wt% concentration showed 72 %, 84 %, 65 %, and 58 % MO dye degradation after 120 min respectively (Fig. 4). Therefore, the result indicates that FeVO4/N, S-CQDs composite synthesized at 0.05 wt% N, S-CQDs doped in FeVO4 exhibit effective photocatalytic activity as compared to other composites [21]. The band gap and surface area of nanoparticles are typical elements influencing their photocatalytic activity. Consequently, the reduced band gap and increased surface area of FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (0.05 wt%), owing to the creation of nanosheet-like formations, can be linked to their improved photocatalytic performance. Fig. 4a displays the plots of (C/Co) versus time for MO dye under UV as well as visible irradiation to give a superior perception of the photocatalytic effectiveness of the materials. In all instances, the change in (C/Co) with time exhibits a linear trend with strong correlation coefficients, suggesting that the MO dye's photocatalytic degradation kinetics [11].

The FeVO4/N, S-CQDs composite exhibits significant visible-light absorption, which is consistent with the literature and suggests an optical band gap of 1.8 eV. This implies that the composite can be activated by visible light. Notably, the optical band gap of 1.8 eV is narrower than that of the pure FeVO4 photocatalyst, which is typically greater than 2.11 eV. As a result, the strong visible-light absorption is expected to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of the composite under visible-light illumination. The optical properties of the sample were analyzed using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, which is a commonly used technique to investigate the electronic state of photocatalytic materials and is extremely useful for photocatalysis [22]. The UV–Vis spectrum of the FeVO4 sample prepared as shown in Fig. 5 was examined, and it was found to have a strong absorption of visible light, consistent with the literature, with an optical band gap of 2.11 eV. Thus, the FeVO4/N, S-CQDs sample prepared in this study can be excited by visible light. The optical band gaps of FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs composite synthesized with different concentrations are determined by UV–visible absorption spectroscopy using the Tauc plot method [23]. The values of the energy band gap Eg for pure FeVO4 and 0.05 wt%, FeVO4/N, S-CQDs 0.10 wt%, FeVO4/N, S-CQDs, and 0.15 wt%, FeVO4/N, S-CQDs composites are 2.04 eV, 2.11 eV, 2.33 eV, and 2.43 eV, respectively, which are consistent with the literature. Fig. 5 indicates that pure FeVO4 shows a lower bandgap energy. However, when N, S-CQDs are added with different concentrations, the bandgap energy increases gradually. This means that when N, S-CQDs are added to FeVO4, the light absorbance ability increases, which enhances photocatalysis. As reported in the literature, the incorporation of N, S-CQDs into the iron vanadate catalyst increases the band gap [24]. Unlike prior reports where the optical band gap of FeVO4 narrows (e.g., to 1.8 eV) upon modification, our samples exhibited an increase in band gap. This discrepancy is likely due to differences in synthesis conditions, improved crystallinity, or the presence of a wider band gap composite material, which can shift the absorption edge. Additionally, reduced defect states or the absence of mid-gap energy levels might contribute to the observed widening.

The optical characteristics of FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs can be identified by UV–visible absorption spectroscopy using the Tauc plot method. The values of the energy band gap Eg for FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs are 2.04 eV and 2.11 eV, respectively, which are very well matched with the previous results. The Tauc plot for pure FeVO4 is presented in Fig. 5a, indicating a band gap of 2.04 eV. The quantum carbon dots have a bandwidth of 2.43 eV. As reported in the literature, the incorporation of carbon quantum dots into the iron vanadate catalyst increases the band gap to 2.11 eV. Using methyl orange dye decolorization, the photocatalytic activity of pure FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposites was investigated. Fig. 4 illustrates the investigation that was conducted on the photocatalytic activity of FeVO4/N, S-CQDs made by breaking down MO in the presence of light [25]. Consequently, light with a wavelength comparable to or greater than the band gap will produce electrons and holes in the presence of N, S-CQDs. When exposed to visible light, they oxidize water molecules on the surface, increasing their photocatalytic effectiveness. In purified FeVO4, the rate of the breakdown of MO is 72 %. In contrast, after 120 min of visible light irradiation in the occupancy of FeVO4/N, S-CQDs, the MO was degraded by 58 % at 0.15 wt% concentration and 65 % at 0.10 wt% concentration. In addition, at 0.05 wt% concentration, the MO was degraded by almost 84 % in 120 min. The FeVO4/N, S-CQDs catalyst has a narrow optical bandwidth and strong visible light absorption. Therefore, the photocatalytic activity of the FeVO4/N, S-CQDs is owing to the strong absorption of visible light. FeVO4 is stimulated by visible light radiation, and the electron-hole pairs are efficiently detached, which contributes to the oxidative decomposition of H2O and organic compounds. Using the decolorization of methyl orange dye, the photocatalytic activity of pure FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposites was examined [26]. The effect of carbon quantum dots on the decomposition of pure photocatalysts under visible light was also investigated. No significant photocatalytic activity was observed with pure FeVO4. The absorption spectrum shows that in the absence of N, S-CQDs, the MO concentration is reduced by only 15 %. Photocatalytic activity is related to peroxide (O2¯) and hydroxyl (•OH) ions. The solution's decolorization is an indicator that the MO dye molecules have broken down. Compared to pure FeVO4, the segregation of photogenerated charge carriers by N, S-CQDs in FeVO4/N, S-CQDs composites is more regulated and enhances electron transfer (electron capture). This will extend the life of heterogeneous recombination, which comprises an increase in terms of photoconversion efficiency. After generating electron-hole pairs in the conduction band (CB) as well as valence band (VB) on the FeVO4 surface, the interaction of visible light causes the electrons to be rapidly transferred to the CQDs (strong electron acceptor) sphere through the percolation mechanism [27]. The electrons reduce oxygen (O2) to form peroxide (O2 –) radicals. Conversely, the holes cause a reaction with water (H2O) by converting catalyst electrons into OH radicals. These reactive species, O2 and •OH, subsequently oxidize the methyl orange (MO) dye molecules absorbed on the FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposite surface, producing carbon dioxide (CO2) and water (H2O) as byproducts. Whereas excessive numbers of N, S-CQDs would cause electron and hole centers of recombination to develop, increasing the ratio of N, S-CQDs did not affect the composite material's photocatalytic performance. This means that photoexcited charges will take part in the photocatalytic reaction decreased by the supplementary recombine process. Therefore, if the content of quantum dots is high, the efficiency of photocatalytic decomposition may be reduced. The energy band gap is an essential parameter that affects the performance of semiconductor photocatalysts. Adsorption, degradation, and release are the main mechanisms involved in the photocatalytic process, where the photo-induced reaction requires the pollutants already absorbed onto the surface of photocatalysts.

The stability, as well as the reusability of photocatalysts, were estimated in order on the measurements of synthesized solutions. The temperature of the solution was maintained at 25 ± 1°C throughout the experiments. The formula for degradation is given as:

Degradation (%)
D
%
=
Co - C t
Co
× 100
The pseudo-first-order kinetic model
ln
Ct
Co
= - k a p p × t
The half-life time of the dye degradation
t 1 / 2 = 0.693 / k a p p
Where D (%) shows the degradation efficiency, Co represents the initial MG concentration, Ct shows the MG concentration at time t, t1/2 is the half-life time of the dye degradation as well and k is the rate constant.

The photocatalytic performance of the iron-based photocatalysts was determined using methyl orange (MO) dyes under visible light irradiation. N, S-CQDs show an essential character in the photocatalytic process owing to excellent charge carrier transfer, photosensitization, as well as up-converted PL (UPCL) effect [28]. N, S-CQDs are most widely used to modify the photocatalytic behavior of the synthesized nanocomposite [29]. The combination of quantum dots tends to broaden the photocatalytic range as well as assemble the separation of charges, that shown to increase the photocatalytic activity [30]. The excitation, as well as the photocatalytic activity of N, S-CQDs, depends upon the conduction band, valence band as well and band gap [31]. The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of scavenges by photocatalysts precedes the pseudo-first-order kinetic model [32]. Table 1 shows the reaction rate constant k, the half-life time t1/2, as well as the degradation (%) obtained from the above equations.

Table 2 shows that N, S-CQDS reveals the highest rate constant, which is higher than that of the FeVO4 sample. The rate constant (k) value of the synthesized nanocomposite is comparable to that of pure materials. The photocatalytic behavior of the synthesized nanocomposite was substantially enhanced than that of other synthesized materials, which shows the interaction between N, S-CQDs as well and FeVO4. The synthesized nanocomposite also shows better visible light absorption abilities and higher photo-generated electron-hole separation capacity than that of pure materials [33]. This result indicates that the MO photodegradation rate has the order FeVO4/ N, S-CQDs > N, S-CQDs > FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (high quantity) > FeVO4. FeVO4/N, S-CQDs (high quantity), and FeVO4 show moderate photocatalytic activity owing to the quick combination of the photogenerated carriers [34]. While N, S-CQDs show higher activity and are very well matched with the optical data. The photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds is a surface-mediated reaction with dye adsorption on the surfaces of photocatalysts [35]. Cationic MO dye can be adsorbed onto the negatively charged nanocomposite, where FeVO4/N, S-CQDs show outstanding photocatalytic behavior than that of other prepared photocatalysts due to their high pore volume. The impact of synthesized samples on the photodegradation of the MO dye is shown in Fig. 6 (a). At the FeVO4/N, S-CQDs, the MO degradation enhanced from 78.44 to 98.66 % because the photoactive positions increased, which tends to increase the degradation efficiency. The increase in nanocomposite dose shows a larger surface area for sample interaction, and the higher concentration of free radicals in the MO solution shows a higher removal percentage of dye [36]. When the dose was further increased, the degradation efficiency dropped to 98.6 % to 80.2 %, respectively. A higher concentration of nanocomposite within the solution can stop the visible light from combining with the reaction suspension and result in a loss of attainable radiation, as well as showing low photoactive sites [30]. The pH factor provides detailed information about the surface charge of the solid that influences the degradation of pollutants, as well as influences the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized nanocomposite. The initial pH of the synthesized solution plays a vital role in the photocatalytic degradation mechanism in an aqueous medium because the photocatalyst as well as the surface charge molecules, are strongly pH dependent. The photocatalytic degradation by pure FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposite with disparate pH of acidic as well as alkaline were investigated to check the pH effects. Fig. 6(b) shows the degradation efficiency of the pure FeVO4 and FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposite for various pH levels, such as pH 3, pH 5 as well and pH 9. The photodegradation efficiency was 39.4 % at pH 3, and it tends to increase to 58.8 % in a less acidic environment having pH 5, and it reduces again to 75.1 % with the increase of pH to 9. However, it decreases again for pure FeVO4, respectively [37].

The effect of this parameter was studied between pH 3 and pH 9. The influence of pH on degradation efficiency for MO dyes can be seen in Fig. 7 (b), which shows a significant degradation at pH 3. The observation established that the percentage of degradation decreased with increasing pH, and as a result, repulsive forces were created. This phenomenon could be attributed to hydroxyl radicals, because an alkaline environment leads to a supplementary source of OH, and the photo-Fenton process promotes the photo reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ [38].

To study the effect of photocatalyst concentration on the degradation mechanism, three solutions of the nanocomposite were synthesized with distilled water in various concentrations, such as 0.25 g/L, 0.5 g/L as well and 1 g/L in the presence of a UV lamp, as mentioned in Fig. 7(a). All these solutions were placed in the dark for 20 min, and this indicates no remarkable removal of dye concentration by adsorption. After that, the solutions were illuminated by a UV lamp having a wavelength of 254 nm, and the optimal degradation occurred at 0.5 g/L owing to the increase in several photogenerated electrons. Above this concentration, the photocatalyst particles agglomeration will occur which impedes the OH radicals from absorbing the visible light [38].

The photocatalytic degradation rate of a dye is dependent on its nature, concentration, as well as on the existing compounds in the water, which are used as a matrix. Fig. 8 represents the effect of the dye’s concentration on the photodegradation efficiency. The degradation efficiencies were determined at various concentrations of MO. The results demonstrate that the optimum degradation was produced at 10 mg/L with a 96.3 % degradation. It can also be seen that the degradation is reduced as the initial concentration increases, or the adsorption of OH on the photocatalyst’s active positions is also reduced, owing to the reduction of light penetration throughout the solution [38].
Error bars and statistical results in Fig. 8: Photocatalytic decoloration of methyl orange with different dye concentrations:

Reusability is a foremost characteristic that is required for photocatalytic applications. The FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposite can be recovered from the dye solutions by the centrifuge method. The efficacy of the prepared FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposite was checked with the addition of MO dye solution, and after 180 min of illumination, the photocatalyst tends to recover under visible light. The reusability of FeVO4 was investigated until the 3rd cycle appeared under the same conditions. After the first photocatalytic test, the photocatalyst was moved apart from the MO solution and centrifuged with distilled water and then dried at 100 °C to be reused. Fig. 7(a) shows the degradation efficiency of MO, it can be seen that the photocatalyst efficiency reduces after the first cycle and it further tends to decrease after the second cycle, and the difference was evaluated at 8.46 % due to the separation of a few Fe-ions from the catalyst surface [39].

FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposites were successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal method with varying concentrations of N, S-CQDs. Structural analysis using XRD confirmed the formation of a pure anorthic phase of FeVO4 with no impurity peaks and a crystallite size of 29 nm. The photocatalyst doped with 0.05 wt% N, S-CQDs exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance, achieving up to 99 % degradation of Methylene Orange under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm). UV–Vis measurements revealed over 80 % degradation using a minimal catalyst dose, while kinetic studies showed pseudo-first-order behavior. SEM analysis indicated a reduction in particle size from 4 µm to 200 nm due to the incorporation of N, S-CQDs, and EDX confirmed the expected elemental composition. The close contact between FeVO4 and carbon quantum dots improved charge separation and enhanced electron transfer. The nanocomposite showed excellent reusability over multiple cycles with no significant loss in activity. A comparison with Methylene Blue also demonstrated strong photocatalytic capability, achieving approximately 90 % degradation within 100 min. These results confirm the potential of FeVO4/N, S-CQDs nanocomposites as efficient, stable, and practical materials for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants in industrial wastewater treatment.

Muhammad Imran Yousaf: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hafsa Mansha: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Umme Habiba: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Misbah Kiran: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Amjed Javid: Visualization, Validation, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis.
